Description: Agent invoking tools, APIs, or system commands beyond authorized scope through misconfiguration, hallucination, or adversarial prompt injection. Root causes: excessive functionality, excessive permissions, excessive autonomy.
Primary Mitigations: PL6 (Agent Boundary Spec), SE5 (Tool Access Control)

Description: User inputs altering agent behaviour in unintended ways, including indirect injection via retrieved documents. Agents with tool access can be hijacked mid-workflow.
LLMs producing plausible but factually incorrect outputs. Distinct from adversarial attacks. Agents acting on hallucinated information cause real damage.

Risk Name: System Prompt and Internal Logic Leakage
Description: Agent system prompts encoding business logic, access controls, or sensitive architecture being extracted by users. Reveals internal rules exploitable to gain higher-level access.

Risk Name: Unbounded Agent Consumption and Cost Escalation
Description: Crippling cloud bills from agents that get stuck in loops. The agent is not hacked – it just never stops.
Secondary Mitigations: SE5 (Tool Access Control), HI3 (Runtime Intervention), OG5 (Performance Monitoring)

Risk Name: Agent Memory Poisoning and State Corruption
Description: Agents that remember things between conversations – until someone corrupts what they remember. A poisoned agent can forget its safety rules or learn harmful behaviours.

Risk Name: Multi-Agent Emergent Behaviour and Covert Coordination
Description: When you connect agents together, they sometimes develop behaviours none were individually instructed to do. One wrong output cascades through the chain.

Risk Name: Agent Skill and Tool Supply Chain Compromise
Description: Add-ons and plugins your agents use are a security blind spot. 26.1% contain vulnerabilities; 5.2% show deliberate malicious intent. Your agent trusts the tool. The tool steals your data.
Secondary Mitigations: OG5 (Performance Monitoring), OG8 (Testing/Auditing), OG9 (Access/Identity Mgmt), PL7 (Supply Chain Assessment)

Description: Five sprawl patterns: functional duplication, shadow agents, orphaned agents, permission creep, unmonitored delegation chains. 96.4% higher incidents in low-governance orgs.
Every agent needs its own login credentials for every system it touches. Machines outnumber humans 80 to 1 in the identity system.
Primary Mitigations: OG9 (Access/Identity Mgmt), SE5 (Tool Access Control), SE6 (Multi-Agent Coordination)

Description: The orchestration layer connecting agents to tools and data stores is a new attack surface. If compromised, all connected agents are controlled.
Your agent passed every test at deployment. Three months later, it produces different results from the same inputs. One test at deployment is not enough.
Secondary Mitigations: HI3 (Runtime Intervention), OG7 (Incident Response), OG14 (Multi-Agent Auditing)

Description: An agent that can write and run code, or browse the web, can be used as a weapon. Agent-driven cyberattack at machine speed.
Primary Mitigations: PL5 (Red-Teaming), PL11 (Skill/Vetting Registry), SE5 (Tool Access Control)

Description: Enterprise agents socially engineered, redirected, or repurposed to defraud the organization's own customers. (AIRI Domain 4.3, 353 incidents)
An agent deployed for a legitimate purpose turns out to be capable of something far more dangerous – and you discover this only after the fact. (AIRI Domain 7.2, 77 entries)
Primary Mitigations: PL5 (Red-Teaming), PL6 (Agent Boundary Spec), OG5 (Performance Monitoring)
Secondary Mitigations: SE5 (Tool Access Control), OG8 (Testing/Auditing), EX5 (Dual-Use Monitoring)

Risk Name: Performance Degradation and Silent Model Regression
Description: The agent that worked perfectly last week now gives noticeably worse answers – and nobody noticed. Degradation is gradual.

Risk Name: Model Bias and Discrimination in Outputs
Description: Agents make worse decisions for some groups than others – not by design, but because training data reflects historical inequalities. (AIRI Domain 1.1, 117 incidents)
Secondary Mitigations: OG4 (Data & Retrieval Monitoring), PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment)

Risk Name: Model Explainability Deficit and Confabulated Reasoning
Description: When the agent makes a decision – can you explain why? The agent will give you a reason, but the reason may itself be made up.

Risk Name: Foundation Model Bias Inheritance and Unauditable Weights
Description: Your agent is built on a model you didn't train and can't fully inspect. You inherit the bias without the ability to fully fix it.

Risk Name: Adversarial Attacks (Prompt Injection Jailbreak Model Extraction)
Description: Deliberate attacks on your agents: tricking them with crafted input, bypassing safety rules, extracting internal instructions.

Risk Name: HITL Oversight Degradation and Automation Bias
Description: The human whose job is to catch agent mistakes becomes the weakest link. After approving 200 correct decisions in a row, they stop checking.
Secondary Mitigations: HI1 (HITL), OG5 (Performance Monitoring), PL8 (AI Literacy Training)

Description: Agents generating false or misleading information without anyone intending it. The output is plausible enough to be trusted. (AIRI Domain 3.1, 174 incidents)
An agent supposed to do X is actually optimizing for Y – and you don't notice because the outputs still look reasonable. (AIRI Domain 7.1)
Agents performing significantly worse for specific demographic groups without discriminatory intent. An effectiveness gap, not value-laden bias. (AIRI Domain 1.3, 33 incidents)
The knowledge base your agent relies on becomes stale, contradictory, or quietly corrupted. Agents may introduce low-quality content back into the library.

Risk Name: Data Bias and Synthetic Feedback Loops
Description: When agents rely on a document library that systematically favours certain viewpoints, their answers inherit that bias. An agent's biased output today becomes tomorrow's training data.

Risk Name: Data Drift and AI Content Contamination
Description: The world the agent was trained on no longer matches the world it's operating in. More of the internet is now AI-generated – like a photocopy of a photocopy.

Risk Name: Data Privacy and Agent Information Leakage
Description: Sensitive information escapes through your agents. Training data containing personal information gets memorized and can be extracted.

Risk Name: Training Data Provenance Copyright and Intellectual Property
Description: Foundation models trained on copyrighted material create legal exposure for downstream deployers. You can't trace what went into the training data.

Risk Name: Control Flow Drifts and Emergent Agent Behaviours
Description: When business rules change, every agent must be updated. More dangerous: agents that improvise, finding paths never reviewed or tested.
Secondary Mitigations: SE5 (Tool Access Control), HI3 (Runtime Intervention), OG6 (Staged Deployments)

Description: The agent gets something wrong, but the wrong answer looks right. Errors cascade through multiple steps silently.
Secondary Mitigations: OG5 (Performance Monitoring), OG7 (Incident Response), SE7 (Confabulation Detection), HI1 (HITL)

Description: Unlike traditional software, agent quality and speed vary with every request. A model update can suddenly double response times – and your SLA covers neither.

Risk Name: Time Lag Effects and Agent Velocity Amplification
Description: When a human makes a mistake, you have minutes. When an agent makes a mistake, it compounds into a catastrophe in seconds. No undo button.
Agent outputs may be less accurate than human judgment. Small per-step errors multiply into large end-to-end failures.
Higher costs to build and maintain agent systems vs. traditional automation due to per-query processing costs, infrastructure overhead, and hidden oversight labour.
Secondary Mitigations: OG3 (Agentic Governance), OG5 (Performance Monitoring), OG12 (Cost Monitoring)

Description: The value of replacing cognitive work is difficult to quantify. When agent and human contributions cannot be separated, investment justification becomes circular.
Knowledge-based work automation via agents threatens a much larger group of employees than RPA alone. One agent-augmented worker replacing 5-10 traditional roles.

Risk Name: Loss of Job Meaning and Professional Identity
Description: Agents taking over creative, judgment-based, and relational work core to employee identity. Meaning displacement leaves only mechanical oversight tasks.
Decision-making is a skill that atrophies when you stop using it. New hires who have never done the work without an agent never develop domain instincts.

Risk Name: Cognitive Work Overload and Agent Oversight Fatigue
Description: Removing simple cognitive work leaves only difficult tasks. Compounded by agent oversight fatigue where humans must monitor multiple agents simultaneously.

Risk Name: Mistrust in Management and Surveillance Spiral
Description: Top-down agent deployment without employee input erodes trust. Agent-based employee surveillance breeds mistrust, reducing cooperation, prompting more surveillance.

Risk Name: Mistrust in Agent Outputs and Confabulated Reasoning
Description: Employees lack trust in agent predictions and reasoning traces – especially when explanations may themselves be confabulations.
Diffusion of accountability across agent chains makes it impossible to identify who is responsible. The 'I was just following the agent's advice' defense becomes pervasive.
Secondary Mitigations: HI5 (Operator Override), HI7 (HITL Monitoring), OG3 (Agentic Governance)

Risk Name: Reduced Work Preparedness and Fallback Readiness Deficit
Description: When agents handle research, analysis, and synthesis, employees spend less time understanding their domain, leaving them unable to step in when agents fail.
Overall process knowledge reduces as agent-based decision chains replace entire business logic pathways. The organization becomes incapable of auditing its own operations.
Secondary Mitigations: SE1 (Explainable AI), HI6 (Intent Communication), OG14 (Multi-Agent Auditing)

Risk Name: Overreliance on AI Agents and Loss of Human Agency
Description: Systematic erosion of human judgment as organizations defer increasingly consequential decisions to agents. (AIRI Domain 5.1/5.2)
AI systems used to monitor, rate, schedule, and discipline workers without recourse. Algorithmic metrics may be gamed, inaccurate, or discriminatory. (AIRI Domain 6.2)
Primary Mitigations: PL3 (Employee Sentiment), HI5 (Operator Override), OG3 (Agentic Governance)

Description: Each individual action your agent takes may be legal on its own. But when multiple agents chain actions together, the combined result can break the law.

Every agent needs its own identity. In a typical enterprise, 80 machine identities for every human identity. Keeping track of who has access to what is the governance problem no organization has solved.
Senior leadership treating agent implementation as purely technical rather than strategic, leading to under-resourcing and unclear accountability.

Risk Name: Public Accountability Gap and Liability Diffusion
Description: When something goes wrong and a member of the public is harmed – who is responsible? Your agent used a model from a vendor and data from a third party.

Risk Name: Agent Liability and Insurance Framework Gap
Description: Standard insurance products (cyber, directors and officers liability, professional indemnity) may not cover agent-caused harms.
Failure of organizational or governmental AI governance structures to keep pace with agentic deployment velocity. (AIRI Domain 6.5)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), PL10 (Safety/Competition Balance), OG3 (Agentic Governance)

Description: Governance structures that exist on paper but do not function in practice. Self-regulation that is cosmetic rather than substantive. (AIRI Domain 6.5)
Commercial model services or agent platforms that become unavailable, degrade, or are discontinued. A single provider outage can affect thousands of downstream deployments.

Risk Name: Third-Party Data and Knowledge Quality Issues
Description: Data and knowledge bases sourced from external parties containing errors, inconsistencies, or adversarial content that degrade agent accuracy.
Over-reliance on a single vendor's proprietary platform, APIs, or model that makes migration costly or infeasible.
Security vulnerabilities or data breaches through third-party agent tools, APIs, or model endpoints.
Secondary Mitigations: SE3 (Guardrails), SE5 (Tool Access Control), OG9 (Access/Identity Mgmt)

Description: Cascading failures from dependencies on multiple third-party agent components. Failure in one vendor system disrupts the entire workflow chain.

Risk Name: Intellectual Property Infringement via Third Parties
Description: Unknowing use of foundation models trained on copyrighted or improperly licensed data, exposing the firm to IP litigation.

Risk Name: Assignment of Liability in Agent Supply Chains
Description: When multiple organizations' agents interact, identifying which agent caused harm becomes intractable.

Risk Name: Departmental Resistance and Shadow Agent Proliferation
Description: Managers and teams resist agentic delegation due to fear of headcount reduction. Unauthorized shadow agents deployed without oversight. Directly feeds OP-AI9 Agent Sprawl.
Valuable employees with AI skills leave for higher pay elsewhere. Those who stay fear they can't compete – or burn out from cleaning up after agents.
Agent-orchestrated financial damage through hallucination-induced contractual breach, unauthorized transactions, runaway spending. Agent velocity amplifies loss magnitude.
Primary Mitigations: PL6 (Agent Boundary Spec), SE5 (Tool Access Control)

Description: Agent-augmented workers operate at multiplicatively higher capacity than unaugmented peers, creating unmanageable performance disparities.

Risk Name: Loss of Control over Agent Operations
Description: Agents that don't stop when you tell them to. They create copies of themselves, schedule future actions autonomously – and you discover they've already set events in motion.
Primary Mitigations: PL6 (Agent Boundary Spec), PL7 (Supply Chain Assessment)
Secondary Mitigations: SE5 (Tool Access Control), HI3 (Runtime Intervention), OG11 (Safety Decision Frameworks)

Risk Name: Competitive Dynamics and AI Race Pressure
Description: Public investment in AI agents triggering competitive arms race, pressuring firms to deploy without adequate safety testing. (AIRI Domain 6.4)

Risk Name: Compensation and Team Design Under AI-Augmented Labor
Description: How to set fair compensation when an AI-augmented worker produces 5x more output. How to structure teams when individual contribution becomes unmeasurable. (AIRI Domain 6.3)
Primary Mitigations: PL3 (Employee Sentiment), PL8 (AI Literacy Training), OG3 (Agentic Governance)

Description: Entire industries relying on the same 2 or 3 model providers or agent platforms. A single failure affects everyone at once.
Primary Mitigations: PL7 (Supply Chain Assessment), EX1 (Industry Consortium), EX4 (Scenario Planning)

Description: Aggregate environmental costs from agent compute infrastructure. (AIRI Domain 5/6.6)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), PL10 (Safety/Competition Balance), EX3 (Public Disclosure)

Description: The deploying firm's agents contribute to real costs that fall on communities, not on the firm's balance sheet. The harm is real. The cost is externalized.
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), EX1 (Industry Consortium), EX3 (Public Disclosure)

Description: AI economic benefits accruing disproportionately to capital owners. Winner-take-most dynamics. (AIRI Domain 6.1, 54 entries)

Risk Name: Digital Colonialism and Global AI Access Divide
Description: AI development concentrated in wealthy nations, imposing values on the Global South. (AIRI Domain 6.1)
AI systems making decisions about people's lives without meaningful consent or recourse. (AIRI Domain 5.2)
Aggregate effects of agent-driven automation on employment structures. (AIRI Domain 6.2/6.3)
Secondary Mitigations: EX1 (Industry Consortium), EX2 (Regulatory Engagement), EX3 (Public Disclosure)

Description: More and more consequential decisions are made by systems that cannot be questioned, appealed, or negotiated with. (AIRI Domain 5.2)
Cumulative pollution of the information commons by millions of agents interacting simultaneously. (AIRI Domain 3.1, 174 incidents)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), SE3 (Guardrails), SE7 (Confabulation Detection), EX3 (Public Disclosure)
Secondary Mitigations: OG3 (Agentic Governance), OG5 (Performance Monitoring), EX1 (Industry Consortium)

Risk Name: Malicious AI-Enabled Disinformation and Democratic Manipulation
Description: Deliberate use of AI agents by bad actors to generate and distribute false information at scale. (AIRI Domain 4.1, 126 incidents)

Risk Name: Algorithmic Curation Drift and Fractured Reality
Description: Not about whether content is true or false – it's about the systems that decide what people see. (AIRI Domain 3.2)
Primary Mitigations: EX1 (Industry Consortium), EX3 (Public Disclosure), EX4 (Scenario Planning)

Risk Name: General Toxic Agent Outputs Polluting the Information Commons
Description: Agents autonomously generating harmful content during normal operation. Diffuse pollution of the information commons. (AIRI Domain 1.2, 87 incidents)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), SE3 (Guardrails), EX3 (Public Disclosure)

Risk Name: AI Safety Alignment Failure at Scale
Description: When agent misalignment happens across the entire ecosystem. A misaligned model used by thousands affects millions. (AIRI Domain 7.1)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), SE2 (Model Alignment), OG11 (Safety Decision Frameworks), EX4 (Scenario Planning)

Description: AI capabilities developed legitimately repurposed for societal harm. The capability frontier advances faster than containment. (AIRI Domain 7.2, 77 entries)
Primary Mitigations: PL10 (Safety/Competition Balance), EX4 (Scenario Planning), EX5 (Dual-Use Monitoring)

Risk Name: Agent-Enabled Fraud Targeting the Public at Scale
Description: Deepfake romance scams, investment fraud, voice cloning at population scale. AIRI's highest-incident subdomain. (AIRI Domain 4.3, 353 incidents)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), SE3 (Guardrails), EX1 (Industry Consortium)

Risk Name: AI-Enabled Political Manipulation of Democratic Processes
Description: Synthetic political content, manufactured consensus, targeted persuasion at electoral scale. (AIRI Domain 4.1)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), SE3 (Guardrails), EX4 (Scenario Planning)

Risk Name: Economic Devaluation of Creative and Cognitive Human Labor
Description: AI-generated art, music, writing, code, and analysis systematically devaluing human creative output at market scale. (AIRI Domain 6.3)

Risk Name: Unfair Discrimination and Allocative Harm at Population Scale
Description: The same groups face worse outcomes across every system simultaneously. The harm is not in any single decision – it's in the pattern. (AIRI Domain 1.1, 117 incidents)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), SE2 (Model Alignment), OG8 (Testing/Auditing)

Risk Name: Toxic and Abusive Agent Outputs Affecting Vulnerable Populations
Description: Agents generating content targeting specific groups with harmful material. Existing moderation systematically fails to protect vulnerable populations. (AIRI Domain 1.2)
Primary Mitigations: PL9 (Societal Impact Assessment), SE3 (Guardrails), EX1 (Industry Consortium)
